retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Upon liberation in plasma, VLDL is degraded to IDL through the hydrolytic action of LPL on the capillary endothelium. LPL acts on VLDL triglycerides via its N-terminal domain, releasing free fatty acids, which are subsequently taken up by adipocytes or myocytes. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipids -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The interaction between water-fearing and fat-loving displays more clearly during lipid transport in plasma. Both cholesterol and triglycerides are nonpolar lipid molecules. Therefore, they must travel in the polar plasma with the help of lipoprotein particles. The main goal of lipoprotein is to help transport lipids (hydrophobic) in water. The structure of lipoprotein consists of triglycerides, cholesterol, phospholipids, and apolipoproteins. Apolipoproteins mainly function as carrier proteins but also serve as cofactors for enzymes that metabolize lipoproteins and help in lipid component exchange among lipoproteins. Some examples of lipoproteins include chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoproteins (HDL). Each one is used in a different phase of lipid transport. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholesterol -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Since cholesterol is mostly a lipophilic molecule, it does not dissolve well in the blood. For this reason, it is packaged in lipoproteins that have phospholipid and apolipoprotein. [3] Lipoproteins are made up of a lipid core (which can contain cholesterol esters and triglycerides) and a hydrophilic outer membrane comprising phospholipid, apolipoprotein, and free cholesterol. This allows the lipid molecules to move around the body through the blood and be transported to cells that need them. There are several types of lipoproteins that travel through the blood, and they each have different purposes. There are high-density lipoproteins (HDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL). Notably, LDL particles are thought to act as a major transporter of cholesterol; at least two-thirds of circulating cholesterol resides in LDL to the peripheral tissues. Conversely, HDL molecules are thought to do the opposite. They take excess cholesterol and return it to the liver for excretion. Clinically, these two lipoproteins are significant since high LDL and low HDL increase patients' risk of atherosclerotic vascular diseases. [4] [5] See Figure. The Molecular Structure of Cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The ApoE isoform ApoE2 is clinically significant due to its reduced affinity for LRP and LDLR. Poor binding of this isoform decreases hepatic clearance of IDL particles and elevates serum triglyceride levels. [6] ApoCIII is considered pathogenic, as it promotes atherosclerosis by delaying the clearance of ApoB-containing lipoproteins and prolonging their presence in plasma. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the postprandial phase, insulin facilitates the transportation of glucose from the bloodstream into cells. [4] Insulin also inhibits gluconeogenesis in the liver and facilitates the storage of glucose in the form of glycogen (glycogenesis) and fats ( de novo lipogenesis (DNL)), which serve as short- and long-term stores of energy, respectively. [4] The human body attempts to maintain homeostasis in blood glucose levels (4 to 6 mmol or about 72 to 108 mg/dL). Homeostasis is influenced by the functional capacity of pancreatic beta-cells and cellular (skeletal muscles, liver, and adipose tissue) sensitivity to insulin. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Ultracentrifugation can also separate IDL particles into 2 subfractions, IDL1 and IDL2. Alternatively, IDL particles may yield 3 midbands, midbands A (MIDA), B (MIDB), and C (MIDC), on electrophoresis. Larger IDL1 (or MIDB/C) particles are triglyceride-rich, whereas smaller IDL2 (or MIDA) particles are cholesterol-rich. Large IDL particles are associated with VLDL and may contribute to an increased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). The functional heterogeneity within lipoprotein fractions likely explains the imperfect correlation between LDL cholesterol levels and the risk of macrovascular diseases. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholesterol -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since cholesterol is mostly lipophilic, it is transported through the blood, along with triglycerides, inside lipoprotein particles (HDL, IDL, LDL, VLDL, and chylomicrons). These lipoproteins can be detected in the clinical setting to estimate the amount of cholesterol in the blood. Chylomicrons are not present in non-fasting plasma.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The Apo C-II in mature VLDL activates LPL in the capillary endothelium of tissues to cleave their stored triglycerides into 1 monoglyceride and 2 free fatty acids, releasing them from the VLDL and allowing them to be delivered to the tissues to be used for storage or energy production. [9] [10] Insulin also upregulates Apo C-II's activity and increases triglyceride hydrolysis into free fatty acids for tissue absorption. Simultaneously, the cholesterol ester transfer protein (CETP) promotes the exchange of VLDL’s triglycerides and phospholipids for HDL’s cholesterol esters. After releasing enough triglycerides either into the tissues or to HDL, the core of the VLDL gets reduced, and the VLDL remnant is now called IDL. Approximately half of the circulating IDL is then taken by the liver by binding to the Apo B-100 and Apo-E. The remaining half of the IDL loses its Apo-E and transforms into LDL through the action of the hepatic lipase.

retrieved_docs.title: Cholestyramine Resin  -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Cholestyramine is a large cationic exchange resin polymer that is insoluble in water. As metabolites of cholesterol, bile acids are normally reabsorbed in the jejunum and ileum efficiently. Excretion is increased up to 10-fold when cholestyramine is given, resulting in the enhanced conversion of cholesterol to bile acids in the liver via 7a-hydroxylation, which is controlled by negative feedback provided by bile acids. Increased LDL and IDL cholesterol uptake from plasma results from the upregulation of the LDL receptors, particularly in the liver. Therefore, the bile acid resins, including cholestyramine, are ineffective in patients with homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia with no functioning receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: IDL particles are formed through the LPL-mediated removal of triglycerides from VLDL. Hepatic lipase subsequently converts these particles to LDL. Thus, IDL represents an essential transition state and determines the cholesterol levels of circulating LDL particles. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Lipids such as cholesterol are insoluble in plasma, and for delivery to tissues such as the adrenal gland, gonads, etc., they have to be packaged into lipoproteins with cholesterol esters and triglycerides in the core and phospholipids, free cholesterol, and apolipoproteins on the surface. Such is the composition of the lipoproteins. [1] Apolipoprotein B (ApoB) is the primary apolipoprotein and is the carrier for the following lipids: chylomicrons, low-density lipoprotein ( LDL), very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoprotein (IDL), and lipoprotein (a).  ApoB is not found in high-density lipoproteins (HDL). The latter are reconstituted into lipoproteins with Apo A.  Hepatic ApoB has a molecular mass of 540000 Da.  There are two circulating forms of Apo B, Apo B48 (from the small intestine) and Apo B100 (from the liver). [1] Intestinal ApoB, which is present in chylomicrons, has a molecular mass of 48% of that of hepatic ApoB.  Hence, hepatic ApoB is termed ApoB100, and intestinal Apo B as apoB 48. The same gene codes for both ApoB48 and ApoB 100.  Apo B-100 contains 4536 amino acids and is necessary for the assembly of VLDL in the liver and also serves as the primary ligand for LDL receptor-mediated clearance of LDL particles from the blood. Apo B-48 has 2512 amino acids and is essential for the formation of chylomicrons, and serves in the absorption of dietary fats from the intestine.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipoprotein Lipase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, LPL plays a significant role in the progression of atherosclerosis. LPL is a component in atherosclerotic lesions, which derive from macrophages. Furthermore, patients with advanced atherosclerosis were found to have elevated LPL mass and activity in their post-heparin plasma. LPL contributes to atherogenic lipoprotein formation. LPL acts on chylomicrons and VLDL to hydrolyze triglycerides from them and form VLDL remnants and chylomicron remnants. VLDL remnants and chylomicron remnants are composed of a high concentration of cholesterol esters and then get incorporated into macrophages. LPL creates free fatty acids, and macrophages then re-esterify them, accumulating cholesterol esters in macrophages, causing macrophages to transform into foam cells. LPL catalyzed the conversion of VLDL to Intermediate density lipoproteins (IDL), which hepatic lipases then convert to low-density lipoprotein (LDL). Foam cells also form when macrophages incorporate oxidized LDL via their scavenger receptor in the vascular endothelium. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipolysis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: TAGs, which provide the body with a significant source of energy, are obtained from the diet or are synthesized endogenously, mainly in the liver. They are transported in the blood as lipoproteins and are stored in adipose tissue. The major classes of blood lipoproteins involved include high-density lipoprotein (HDL),  intermediate-density lipoprotein (IDL), low-density lipoprotein (LDL), very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), and chylomicrons. Chylomicrons undergo synthesis in the small intestine and transport dietary TAGs from the small intestine to tissues such as muscle and adipose. The liver synthesizes VLDLs in the liver and transport TAGs from the liver to tissues in the same way. HDLs have multiple functions relative to lipid metabolism, including serving an integral role in the conversions of VLDL to LDL. HDL also serves as a reservoir for essential apoproteins such as Apo C-II. Apo C-II activates lipoprotein lipase, the enzyme responsible for digestion and breakdown of TAGs. Synthesis of TAGs stores in adipose tissue occurs in the fed state after a meal.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Low Density Lipoprotein -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Both cholesterol esters (CE) and triacylglycerols (TG) are insoluble in water (plasma); hence they are packaged as lipoproteins comprising an inner core of CE and TG with a surface coat of apolipoproteins, free cholesterol (FC), and phospholipids. Packaged as lipoproteins, they can be transported to various tissues where needed. By differing the lipid and protein concentrations, up to 5 different lipoproteins can be produced, such as chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoproteins (HDL). LDL is the predominant carrier of serum cholesterol (around 67%) and delivers it to tissues of need such as the adrenal glands, gonads, and other tissues. With a density of 1.019 to 1.063 g/ml, LDL contains 20% protein and 50% cholesterol (CE and FC) and displays beta mobility on electrophoresis. This review focuses on LDL, especially its biochemistry, measurement, and clinical significance. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: IDL particles exhibit a reduced FCR and prolonged plasma residence in patients with metabolic syndrome. [39] Such tendencies may explain the association between IDL cholesterol levels and the progression of metabolic syndrome. [40] Lipid-lowering drugs, such as fibrates and statins, can enhance the FCR of IDL particles, lowering both IDL and LDL cholesterol levels. Therefore, these agents may be used to manage dyslipidemia in metabolic syndrome. [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dyslipidemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cholesterol and TG are the two most relevant plasma lipids. Because lipids are insoluble in water, they are transported in the blood with the help of lipoproteins. They constitute the hydrophobic core of lipoproteins separated from plasma by phospholipoproteins and apolipoproteins. Lipoproteins are classified into chylomicrons (CM), very low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoprotein (HDL). Plasma lipoproteins are subclassified based on their size, density, and composition, as listed in the table. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperosmolality and hypernatriemia are classified as part of the broader category of other disorders of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance, reflecting their role as disturbances in plasma osmolality and sodium concentration that impact overall homeostatic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1298
retrieved_docs.content: INSR protein, human is the molecular target of insulin aspart, human, which serves as the active moiety in the formulation insulin aspart protamine, human 70 UNT/ML / insulin aspart, human 30 UNT/ML Injectable Suspension. Insulin aspart, human is employed in the management of both مرض السكري نوع 1 and مرض السكري نوع 2, including مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين. Clinically, ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما is associated with مرض السكري نوع 1, مرض السكري نوع 2, and insulin‑requiring مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين, as well as with حالة نفسية غير طبيعية. Additionally, ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما frequently co‑occurs with قلة حجم الدم. These interconnections underscore the therapeutic role of insulin aspart in diverse diabetic populations and highlight the importance of monitoring plasma osmolality, volume status, and neuropsychiatric conditions in patients receiving insulin therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1687
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), indicating that patients with this form of diabetes are at heightened risk for sudden deterioration of renal function. In the context of acute kidney failure, abnormalities in extrarenal nitrogenous blood (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية) may develop, reflecting the accumulation of nitrogenous waste products when the kidneys are unable to adequately filter the blood. Moreover, acute kidney failure serves as a parent condition for urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), suggesting that severe renal impairment can predispose patients to complications of the urinary system. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of vigilant monitoring of renal health in insulin‑dependent type 2 diabetic patients, early detection of nitrogenous waste disturbances, and proactive management of potential urinary tract complications to improve overall patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic landscape for diabetes is organized into two principal categories: insulins and analogues, and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Within the insulin subclass, formulation groups are defined. “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting” comprises rapid‑acting analogue insulin aspart (human), which binds the human insulin receptor (INSR) and produces the physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting an anabolic action on adipose tissue. “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting” includes insulin glargine and the fixed‑dose combinations insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide; both combinations integrate a basal insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue to enhance glycaemic control. “Insulin, Beef” is a protein entity classified within the same combined intermediate‑/long‑acting‑with‑fast‑acting group, illustrating the diversity of insulin sources.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 197
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart (human) exerts a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis and acts on the INSR protein (human) as a molecular target. This rapid‑acting insulin analogue is employed in the treatment of both مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes) and مرض السكري نوع 2 (type 2 diabetes). Clinical observations link both نوع 1 and نوع 2 diabetes to an increased risk of فشل كلوي حاد (acute kidney failure) and to ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما (elevated plasma osmolality). Acute kidney failure frequently co‑occurs with dehydration (التجفاف), abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية), low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), and is especially associated with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين). Elevated plasma osmolality is also clinically associated with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes and abnormal mental states, and it commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume. Dehydration is a physiological precursor that maps to elevated plasma osmolality, reflecting the close relationship between fluid loss and osmotic imbalance. Both فشل كلوي حاد and ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما are categorized under اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder), indicating that they are specific manifestations within the broader spectrum of urinary tract pathology. Together, these interconnections highlight how insulin therapy, diabetes, renal dysfunction, fluid‑electrolyte disturbances, and neuro‑psychiatric manifestations are intertwined, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management in patients with diabetes‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with dehydration (التجفاف) and with abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). When renal function declines sharply, the condition can give rise to extrarenal blood nitrogen accumulation (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), reflecting the buildup of nitrogenous waste products that the kidneys are no longer able to clear. These interrelated clinical features highlight the importance of recognizing fluid deficits and neuropsychiatric changes as potential indicators of acute kidney injury and of monitoring nitrogen waste levels to guide timely management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2090
retrieved_docs.content: INSR protein, human serves as the molecular target for insulin aspart, human, indicating that the rapid‑acting insulin analogue binds to the human insulin receptor to exert its glucose‑lowering effect. The pharmaceutical formulation insulin aspart protamine, human 70 UNT/ML / insulin aspart, human 30 UNT/ML Injectable Suspension contains insulin aspart, human as its active moiety, combining protamine‑bound and free insulin aspart to provide both basal and prandial insulin coverage. Clinically, insulin aspart, human is employed in the management of both مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes) and مرض السكري نوع 2 (type 2 diabetes), reflecting its utility across the major forms of diabetes mellitus. Understanding the interaction between insulin aspart and INSR protein, human, together with the composition of its suspension product, guides appropriate therapeutic use in diabetic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 37
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human insulin analogue, is classified within the group “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting.” This class also includes beef insulin, indicating that both insulin aspart and insulin, beef are members of the same therapeutic category of injectable diabetes treatments. Insulin aspart exerts its action by targeting the human insulin receptor (INSR protein) and produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting its role in suppressing the breakdown of fat. Together, these relationships highlight how insulin aspart functions at the molecular level and its placement among other injectable insulin preparations used in diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1932
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم) and with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين). In addition, acute kidney failure has a sub‑entity of extrarenal nitrogen accumulation (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that nitrogenous waste may build up outside the kidneys. These interconnections emphasize that hypovolemia and poorly controlled insulin‑dependent type 2 diabetes are important risk factors or co‑morbid conditions for acute kidney failure, and that monitoring extrarenal nitrogen levels is relevant for its diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2185
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES is a broad therapeutic category that encompasses insulins and analogues. Insulins and analogues represent a specific subclass of drugs within this category, providing essential hormone replacement and glucose‑lowering effects for patients with diabetes. Within the insulins and analogues subclass, the formulation described as Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate- or long-acting combined with fast-acting is a further specialized group. This group consists of injectable insulin preparations that combine intermediate‑ or long‑acting basal insulin with rapid‑acting insulin to achieve both steady background glucose control and prompt post‑prandial reduction. Understanding this hierarchical relationship clarifies how the specific injection regimen fits into the larger class of insulin therapies and the overall spectrum of drugs used in diabetes, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment strategies for optimal glycemic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2062
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin glargine and the combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide are both classified as members of the therapeutic group “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting.” This category comprises long‑acting injectable insulin analogues that are used to provide basal insulin coverage in the management of diabetes, helping to maintain stable blood glucose levels over extended periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders are closely linked to the development of diabetic hyperosmolar coma, as disturbances in fluid balance and electrolyte concentrations can precipitate the severe hyperosmolar state that characterizes this life‑threatening complication of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2745
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran is a branched glucose polymer used in medicine as a plasma volume expander, antithrombotic agent, and drug‑delivery carrier. Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight (approximately 40 kDa) preparation, is a specific type of dextran and is commonly employed for short‑term intravascular volume expansion and to reduce blood viscosity during surgical procedures. Recognizing that dextran 40 is a subset of the broader dextran family guides clinicians in selecting the appropriate formulation based on desired pharmacologic effects and safety considerations.
